name: San Sebastián Gastronomika
tagline: The world's chefs descend on the Basque food capital while the Parte Vieja's pintxos counters groan under edible miniatures.
dates: October 5–7, 2026 (confirmed for the 28th edition; pintxos bars are at their best all week around it)
history: San Sebastián Gastronomika traces its lineage to 1999, when food critic Rafael García Santos launched 'Lo Mejor de la Gastronomía' in San Sebastián — widely regarded as the first international haute-cuisine congress in the world. The format it pioneered, chefs demonstrating live on stage to an auditorium of their peers, became the template for every food summit that followed, from Madrid Fusión to MAD in Copenhagen. In 2009 the event was relaunched as San Sebastián Gastronomika under Roser Torras's Grup GSR together with the local daily El Diario Vasco (Vocento group), and moved definitively into the Kursaal Congress Centre, Rafael Moneo's luminous glass-cube landmark on Zurriola beach.

The setting is no accident. San Sebastián and the surrounding Gipuzkoa hills hold one of the highest densities of Michelin stars per capita on Earth, the legacy of the 1970s New Basque Cuisine (Nueva Cocina Vasca) movement led by Juan Mari Arzak and Pedro Subijana, who took inspiration from French nouvelle cuisine and applied it to Basque tradition. Gastronomika is that movement's annual family reunion: three days of stage demos, tastings, wine sessions, and awards where legends like Arzak, Subijana, Berasategui and Aduriz share billing with rising stars from a rotating guest country. Editions have paid tribute to everyone from Ferran Adrià to Japanese kaiseki masters, and its Gueridón de Oro and homage awards are career-capping honors in Spanish gastronomy.

The 2026 edition (October 5–7) is a landmark one: under the slogan 'The spark that lit the fire,' the 28th congress celebrates the 50th anniversary of New Basque Cuisine, with confirmed appearances by Pierre Gagnaire, Michel Troisgros and Gastón Acurio honoring the 1976 revolution that reshaped Spanish cooking. Even if you never set foot in the Kursaal, congress week electrifies the city — the world's food press, chefs and sommeliers spill nightly into the pintxos bars of the Parte Vieja, and you may find a three-star chef elbow-to-elbow with you over a plate of grilled txuleta.
whyGo: This is the densest concentration of culinary talent in Europe in the city best equipped to feed it — a congress founded on this very stage in 1999, held in a Moneo-designed landmark, during a week when the pintxos bars of the old town fill with the world's best chefs off duty. The 2026 edition marks 50 years of New Basque Cuisine, arguably the most consequential anniversary in modern Spanish food. Come for the congress if you're a professional; come for the atmosphere and eating even if you're not.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Bilbao (BIO) direct with Edelweiss (WK226, ~5x weekly) or Vueling, then Ekialdebus/PESA DO50 coach from Bilbao Airport direct to San Sebastián bus station
gettingThere.cost: CHF 120–300 return + €17 each way for the bus
gettingThere.notes: The airport coach runs roughly hourly and drops you at San Sebastián's central bus station; buy on board or via the Alsa/PESA app. Book the flight early — congress week fills fast.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Biarritz (BIQ) via Paris, or Zürich → Madrid + connection to San Sebastián (EAS); Biarritz is 50 km away with bus links via Hendaye
gettingThere.notes: Fallback if Bilbao fares spike. From Biarritz, take the bus/train to Hendaye then the Euskotren 'Topo' metro across the border into San Sebastián (~35 min, ~€3).

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Paris Gare de Lyon (TGV Lyria, 4h05) → Paris Montparnasse → Hendaye (TGV, ~4h40) → Euskotren Topo to Donostia/Amara
gettingThere.duration: 11–12h total; feasible in a day or overnight in Paris
gettingThere.notes: The flight-free option. Book TGV Lyria and SNCF legs ~3 months out for the low fare buckets; the Topo border metro needs no reservation.

costs.notes: Assumes flights from Zürich, 3 nights, and eating out every meal. San Sebastián hotels are among Spain's priciest and congress week compounds it — the bed is the biggest variable (pensión ~€80–120/night vs. Maria Cristina €450+). A pintxos crawl runs €30–50/evening; the full Gastronomika professional pass is several hundred euros; a 3-star tasting menu at Arzak or Akelarre adds €250–400 per person.

weather: Early October in San Sebastián is mild (14–20°C) but famously wet — the Bay of Biscay delivers sudden Atlantic showers between spells of golden autumn light. Pack a proper rain jacket and shoes with grip for wet old-town cobbles; a light sweater covers the evenings. Sea is still swimmable for the brave (~19°C).
tips: The congress itself is professional-oriented — check which sessions and the popular-program tastings are open to the public before buying; if you just want the vibe, the city's bars during congress week are the real free show.

Book your hotel the moment you commit — congress week plus the tail of film-festival season makes this the tightest hotel market in northern Spain. Consider staying in Gros (near the Kursaal, better value, great pintxos of its own).

Pintxos etiquette: do a crawl, not a sit-down — one or two pintxos and a small drink (zurito of beer or a txikito of wine) per bar, then move on. Order the cooked specials from the blackboard, not just the counter display; that's where the kitchens show off.
Reserve the big tables months ahead: Arzak, Akelarre and Martín Berasategui book out 2–3 months in advance for October, and congress week compresses availability further.
Go early or late: pintxos bars in the Parte Vieja are crushed 13:00–15:00 and 20:30–22:30 during congress week. Arrive at opening (~12:30 or 19:30) to actually reach the bar.

Money-saver: lunch menús del día at serious restaurants (€25–40) deliver much of the destination's cooking at a third of the tasting-menu price, and the DO50 airport bus at €17 beats a €120+ taxi from Bilbao.

localRecs.name: Bar Néstor
localRecs.note: Tiny Parte Vieja legend — one tortilla at 13:00 and 20:00 (put your name on the list an hour ahead), txuleta steak, and the famous tomato salad. Cash-friendly, zero pretension.

localRecs.name: La Cuchara de San Telmo
localRecs.note: No counter display — everything cooked to order from the blackboard. The braised veal cheek in red wine and the foie with apple compote are the reference orders.

localRecs.name: Ganbara
localRecs.note: Pintxos bar upstairs, asador downstairs; in early October its mound of wild mushrooms (hongos) with egg yolk is peak-season and arguably the single best plate in the old town.

localRecs.name: Parte Vieja
localRecs.note: The old town between the harbour and the Urumea — the densest concentration of pintxos bars in the world, all within ten minutes' stumble of each other. Calle 31 de Agosto is the spine.

localRecs.name: Monte Igueldo funicular
localRecs.note: Creaky 1912 funicular to the belle-époque viewpoint over La Concha bay — the classic postcard panorama, best an hour before sunset between eating shifts.

photos.caption: A pintxos counter in San Sebastián — the edible skyline that fills every bar of the Parte Vieja
A classic old-town bar in Donostia, laden with pintxos and hanging hams
The Kursaal Congress Centre by Rafael Moneo on Zurriola beach — home of Gastronomika since 2009
